concepts.title: Oscillatory and Periodic Motion
concepts.conceptBody:
Oscillatory and Periodic Motion

Oscillatory Motion : It a body moves to and fro on the same path about a mean position, it is called as oscillatory motion. For example : Swinging pendulum, vibratory motion of a mass attached to a spring.

Periodic Motion : A motion which repeats itself after equal intervals of time is called a periodic motion. Each oscillatory motion is a periodic motion, but every periodic motion is not oscillatory. For example, a body moving in a circle is an example of periodic motion but it is not oscillatory motion.

concepts.title: SHM of a Particle
concepts.conceptBody:
Simple Harmonic Motion 

It is a special type of periodic oscillatory motion in which :

(i) Particle oscillates on a straight line.

(ii) The acceleration of the particle is always directed towards a fixed point on the line.

(iii) The magnitude of acceleration is proportional to the displacement of the particle from the fixed point.

This fixed point is called the mean position. Now, from the definition, we can write the equation of simple harmonic motion as,

$\text{a}=-{{\omega }^{2}}\text{x}$

Where ${{\omega }^{2}}$ is a positive constant.

The above figure shows a basic example of SHM. The particle starts from O towards B with some velocity. Each moment the acceleration of the particle is directed towards point O. Hence, due to this negative acceleration the velocity of the particle becomes zero at $\text{B}$ and it starts gaining velocity as it goes from $\text{B}$ to $\text{O}$. The value of acceleration becomes zero when it reaches point $\text{O}$. This motion is again repeated.

Hence, this type of motion is called simple harmonic motion.

concepts.title: Kinematics of SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
The Cases of Oscillation 

Consider a particle free to move on x-axis, is being upon by a force given by,

$\text{F}=-k{{x}^{\text{n}}}$

Here, $\text{k}$ is a positive constant.

Now, following cases are possible depending on the value of $n$.

(i) If $\text{n}$ is even integer $(0,2,4,\ldots $ etc.), force is always along negative $\text{x}$-axis, whether $\text{x}$ is a positive or negative. Hence, the motion of the particle is not oscillatory. If the particle is released from any position on the $\text{x}$-axis (except at $\text{x}=0$ ) a force in negative direction of $\text{x}$ axis acts on it and it moves rectilinearly along negative $x$-axis.

(ii) If $\text{n}$ an odd integer $(1,3,5\ldots $ etc.), force is along negative $\text{x}$-axis for $\text{x}>0$, along positive $\text{x}$-axis for $\text{x}<0$ and zero for $\text{x}=0$. Thus, the particle will oscillate about stable equilibrium position, $x=0$. The force in this case is called the restoring force. Of these, if $n=1$, i.e. $F$ $=-\text{kx}$ the motion is said to be SHM.

concepts.title: Equation of SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of Simple Harmonic Motion 

The necessary and sufficient condition for a motion to be simple harmonic (Linear) is

\(\begin{gathered}\mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{kx} \\ \text { i.e. m } \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{kx} \\ \text { or } \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt} \mathrm{t}^2}=-\omega^2 \mathrm{x} \text { with } \omega^2=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}}\end{gathered}\)

The solution of the above equation will be seen in a chapter called differential equations in mathematics.

Hence, we are directly proceeding to the answer of this equation.

Which is,

$\text{x}=\text{Asin }\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

concepts.title: Amplitude of SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
Amplitude of SHM

It is the maximum displacement of the particle from its mean position.

Equation (1) gives the displacement of the particle. The value of ' $\text{x}$ ' is maximum when sin $\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$ is maximum i.e., $\text{sin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\pm 1$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{\text{max}}}=\text{A}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ A is the amplitude of simple harmonic motion.

concepts.title: Phase in SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
Phase in SHM

The quality $\theta =\omega t+\phi $ is called the phase. It determines the state of the particle in simple harmonic motion.

When the particle is at mean position, $\text{x}=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=0 & {} \\ \therefore & \omega \text{t}+\phi =\text{n}\pi ; & \text{n}=0,1,2,3,\ldots . \\ \end{array}$

(i) consider $\text{n}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \omega \text{t}+\phi =0$

$\therefore \text{x}=0$

and $\text{v}=\omega \text{Acos}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\omega \text{A}$

i.e., the particle is crossing the mean position and is moving towards the positive direction.

(ii) consider $\text{n}=1$ 
$\therefore $ $\omega \text{t}+\phi =\pi $ 
$\therefore $ $\text{x}=0$ 
and $\text{v}=-\omega \text{A}$

i.e., again the particle is crossing the mean position but now it is moving towards the negative direction.

When the particle is at extreme position , $x={{x}_{\text{max}}}$

i.e., $\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\pm \text{A}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{3\pi }{2},\ldots $ 

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\frac{\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)}{2}\pi ;\text{n}=0,1,2\ldots $ 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ consider $\text{n}=1$

,

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\omega \text{t}+\phi =\frac{3\pi }{2}$ 

 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=-\text{A}$

 

and $\text{v}=0$

i.e., the particle is at extreme left and again its velocity is zero.

From above it is clear that as time increases the phase increases. An increases of $2\pi $ brings the particle to the same status in the motion. Thus, a phase $\omega t+\phi $ is equivalent to a phase Similarly, the acceleration of the particle is given by,

$\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{x}}{\text{d}{{\text{t}}^{2}}}=-{{\omega }^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

It is zero when phase $\left( \omega t+\phi \right)=0$ and maximum $\left( {{\omega }^{2}}A \right)$ when phase $\left( \omega t+\phi \right)=\left( \frac{2n+1}{2} \right)\pi $.

Graphically the variation of position, velocity and acceleration with the phase is shown below.

concepts.title: Phase Constant
concepts.conceptBody:
Phase Constant

The constant term $\phi $ in the equation (3) is called phase constant or initial phase or epoch of the particle. This constant depends on the choice of the instant $t=0$

Suppose we choose $t=0$ at an instant when the particle is passing through its mean position towards right (i.e. positive direction). Then the phase $\theta =\omega \text{t}+\phi $ has to be zero. Since $t=0$ this means $\phi =0$. So the equation of displacement becomes

$\text{x}=\text{Asin}\omega \text{t}$

If we choose $t=0$ when the particle is at its extreme position in the positive direction. The phase $\theta =\frac{\pi }{2}$ at this instant and hence $\phi =\frac{\pi }{2}$. Therefore equation of displacement becomes

$\text{x}=\text{Acos}\omega \text{t}$

The sine form and cosine form are basically equivalent. The value of phase constant, however, depends on the form chosen; for example

$\begin{matrix} \text{x}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\frac{\pi }{2}+{\phi }' \right) \\ \text{{x}'}=\text{Acos}\left( \omega \text{t}+{\phi }' \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Displacement, Velocity and Acceleration of SHM
concepts.conceptBody:
Displacement, Velocity and Acceleration of SHM

1. Position 

It mean position is at origin, the position $\left( \text{x} \right)$ depends on time in general as :

$x\left( t \right)=A\text{sin}\left( \omega t+\phi \right)$

Thus,

(1) at mean position, $x=0$

(2) at extremes, $x=+A,-A$

2.Velocity 

The expression of velocity at any time instant $t$.

as

$\begin{matrix} \text{x}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right) \\ \text{V}\left( \text{t} \right)=\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=\text{Awcos}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

From equation 1,

$\begin{matrix} \text{sin}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\frac{\text{x}}{\text{A}} \\ \text{ or }\text{ cos}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{A}}^{2}}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{\text{ A}}} \\ \end{matrix}$

using equation (3) in (1)

$\therefore \text{ V}\left( \text{x} \right)=+\omega \sqrt{{{\text{A}}^{2}}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

Also,

(1) Velocity is minimum at extremes because the particle is at rest. i.e. $\text{V}=0$ at extreme position $\left( \text{x}+\text{A} \right)$

(2) Velocity has maximum magnitude at mean position

$|\text{V}{{|}_{\text{max}}}=\text{ wA at }\text{ mean }\text{ position }\left( \text{x}=0 \right)$

3. Acceleration : 

As $\text{v}=\text{A}\omega \text{cos}\left( \omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

and $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}$

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{a}=-\omega^2 \mathrm{A} \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}+\phi)$

or at any position x, $\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{x})=-\omega^2 \mathrm{x}$

(1) Acceleration is always directed towards the mean position

(2) Magnitude of acceleration is minimum at mean position and maximum at extremes.

$|\text{a}{{|}_{\text{min}}}=0\text{ at }\text{ mean }\text{ position }$

$|\mathrm{a}|_{\max }=\omega^2 \mathrm{A}$ at extremes
